title: Washington Crossing the Delaware
note: Painted in Düsseldorf, seventy-five years after the fact, by a German who had never seen the river. The flag in the boat did not exist yet. Patriotic painting as fan fiction.

title: Aristotle with a Bust of Homer
note: Aristotle is wearing seventeenth-century Dutch clothes and thinking about a Greek bust an art dealer in Messina sold to Don Antonio Ruffo. The chain on Rembrandt's invoice is the chain on the painting.

title: Self-Portrait with a Straw Hat
note: He painted himself thirty-five times. The straw hat is Paris, before he went south to Arles and stopped sleeping. The mirror has reversed the part in his hair.

title: Wheat Field with Cypresses
note: Painted from inside the asylum at Saint-Rémy. He told Theo the cypresses were the one thing he had not yet conquered. He had a year left to try.

title: Shoes
note: Heidegger wrote a famous essay calling these a peasant woman's shoes. Meyer Schapiro proved they were van Gogh's own. Famous philosophers vs footwear: 0–1.

title: Midas Washing at the Source of the Pactolus
note: Midas got the wish, hated it, and came here to wash it off. The Pactolus runs in present-day Türkiye, and locals will still tell you the gold flecks are real.

title: The Spanish Singer
note: Manet's first painting the Salon accepted. Spain was fashionable in 1860s Paris — tourism by oil. The cigarette never gets shorter.

title: Venice, from the Porch of Madonna della Salute
note: Turner went to Venice three times. He painted it for the next twenty years. The light is exactly right; the buildings are approximately right. Both were the point.

title: Circus Sideshow
note: Pointillism is a theory. This is the theory under stress test — every dot is a fortieth-thousand commitment to the same colour choice. Seurat died four years later, at thirty-one.

title: Still Life with Apples and a Pot of Primroses
note: He said he wanted to astonish Paris with an apple. He painted apples for forty years. Paris was duly astonished.

title: Ia Orana Maria
note: Gauguin's first major Tahiti work. A Polynesian Madonna with two angels, none of whom asked to be Catholic. He saw what he wanted to see, but he saw it well.

title: Sunflowers
note: Paris, not Arles. Four cut sunflowers on a table. The famous yellow series came two years later, in the south, with Gauguin and the Yellow House and the ear.

title: La Berceuse
note: The postman's wife, Augustine Roulin. He painted the whole Roulin family — they fed him in Arles when no one else would. The flowered backdrop is what he saw when his eyes closed.

title: Rubens, Helena Fourment
note: Rubens was fifty-three. Helena was sixteen. The painting is a family portrait the way a Mar-a-Lago Christmas card is a family portrait — staged with one person's pleasure in mind.

title: The Lamentation
note: Counter-Reformation art on a deadline. The Council of Trent had ruled out ambiguity in religious painting. Pulzone delivered: the dead Christ is unambiguous.

title: Still Life with Peaches and Grapes
note: Renoir rarely did still life. Painted on the trip where he met Cézanne in Provence. The fruit was probably eaten by someone in the studio that night.

title: Portrait of a Girl
note: Sampson Roch was deaf. He painted miniatures for aristocrats' lockets in Bath. The girl is anonymous now; the locket is presumably lost; only the painter's name survived.

title: Young Woman with a Water Pitcher
note: Thirty-four Vermeers exist. The window light here is the same light that made Delft photographers good a hundred years before there were photographers.

title: Allegory of the Catholic Faith
note: Vermeer's only allegory. He converted to Catholicism for marriage; this was the receipt. Symbols stacked like footnotes you can almost read.

title: Mars and Venus United by Love
note: Mars looks distracted, Venus looks radiant. The horse is unimpressed. Cupids tying the lovers' legs together is, apparently, the iconographic shorthand for marriage.

title: Under the Wave off Kanagawa
note: It is a print, not a painting — one of thirty-six. Mount Fuji is small in the trough. Hokusai is showing you what the wave thinks of mountains.

title: Hunter with Hounds
note: Porcelain, not paint. Höchst made trinkets that aristocrats put on shelves. Two and a half centuries later they are in the Met. Time chose for them.

title: The Farm at Les Collettes, Cagnes
note: Renoir bought this farm for the climate. He had rheumatism so bad he tied the brush to his hand. The olive trees are still there. The brushes are not.

title: Boy with a Sword
note: Léon Leenhoff, age nine — officially Manet's wife's brother, unofficially his son. The sword is a studio prop. The admiration of Velázquez is real.

title: Saint Catherine of Alexandria in Prison
note: Saint Catherine reading in prison before her execution. Veronese was very good at painting people who were calmer than their situation required.

title: Christ Presented to the People
note: Sodoma was a nickname — a slur he embraced as a brand four hundred years before that was a marketing strategy. He out-painted the slur.

title: Madame Roulin and Her Baby
note: The Roulin family fed Vincent in Arles when no one else would. He painted all of them. This is the third portrait of Augustine and her baby Marcelle.

title: The Potato Peeler
note: Pre-Paris, pre-colour. Dark Dutch palette. He was painting the De Groot family in Nuenen and learning that hands work harder than faces.

title: Portrait of a Woman
note: We do not know who she is. Rembrandt's account books are missing some pages. The face is the proof; the name is a casualty of bookkeeping.

title: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
note: Five years, about a quarter of a million dots. Scientific colour theory applied with monastic discipline. Sondheim later made it a musical. Seurat died at thirty-one.

title: The Bedroom
note: Three versions exist. This is the second, painted at Saint-Rémy. The first was made in Arles waiting for Gauguin to arrive at the Yellow House. Gauguin arrived. It went badly.

title: Paris Street; Rainy Day
note: Photographic before photography was art. The pavers are wet from a real rain. Haussmann had just rebuilt this part of Paris; the bourgeoisie needed new pavement to walk on.

title: Water Lilies
note: One of about two hundred and fifty water-lily paintings. The Giverny pond was small; the obsession was not. By 1906 his eyes had cataracts. Some of the colour is the cataracts.

title: Two Sisters (On the Terrace)
note: They were not sisters. Renoir liked the title. The title is a compliment, not a fact. Most Impressionist titles are compliments.

title: The Beach at Sainte-Adresse
note: Monet's family had a villa here. He came back from the war, painted the beach, was not yet Monet. He was almost Monet.

title: Henri Degas and His Niece Lucie Degas (The Artist's Uncle and Cousin)
note: Painted in Naples, with the Italian uncle and the eight-year-old niece. Degas painted his family the way he painted his ballerinas — privately, but with proof.

title: Stacks of Wheat (End of Summer)
note: One of twenty-five paintings of the same two haystacks. Monet invented the series. After this, you could no longer paint a thing once and call it done.

title: The Poet's Garden
note: He thought of Petrarch and Boccaccio walking through this Arles park. He saw Italy in Provence because he wanted to. Painters do that.

title: Woman Reading
note: Reading-while-female-and-modern was a Manet specialty. Beer in front of her, book in hand. The Salon was scandalised by the beer.

title: The Herring Net
note: Two men, one boat, one net, more fish than expected. Homer had just moved from magazine illustration to painting. The wave behind them is doing what waves do.

title: The Millinery Shop
note: Working-class women making hats for upper-class women. Degas drew this shop for seven years. The hats are arranged like buildings; this is no accident.

title: The Bay of Marseille, Seen from L'Estaque
note: Cézanne sat on this hill for years. He kept painting the bay until it made sense to him as geometry. Cubism started here, before anyone noticed.

title: Woman at Her Toilette
note: Morisot was the only woman in the original Impressionist circle the men took seriously. She painted mirrors a lot. Make of that what you like.

title: Physician's Tray
note: Faience tray, made to hold a doctor's instruments. The decorative motif is hopeful. The instruments were eighteenth-century. Choose your prayer accordingly.

title: Self-Portrait
note: Another of the thirty-five self-portraits. This one is dotted — he had just seen Seurat. He tried everything once before he ran out of time.

title: The Crucifixion
note: The light source is offstage and theological. Zurbarán's Christ steps out of the dark like a slide projector at the wrong end of the room. Spanish Catholicism in one image.

title: Portrait of a Young Lady
note: Dutch Golden Age portraiture. The lace alone took a craftsman a year. The young lady's name is lost. The lace is still on the painting.
